notes: 5,000 employees worldwide.
Mid-market IT budget range.
IT viewed as cost center, not strategic asset. Contrast with State Street (CEO). Drives cost-reduction focus.
Primary data center.
All Timex locations combined.
At stated maximum threshold; no slack capacity for repurposing. Consistent with survey finding of 65% having >60% utilization.
Moderate growth; both Windows and Unix expected to grow 10%.
Same as Windows.
Dominant storage interface; both DAS and SAN.
Residual legacy SCSI.
Extraordinarily high; examples: data warehouse, watch images, OLTP (written-once), CAD/CAM post-release. Despite this, willingness to tier is very low.

metric_name: Willingness: higher capacity + 50% cost reduction + slight availability reduction (7-point scale)
notes: Very low willingness despite high inactive data percentage. Administrative complexity is the barrier — not cost or availability.

metric_name: Willingness: same capacity + 30% cost reduction + slight availability reduction (7-point scale)
notes: Equally low. Both options rejected. Consistent barrier: doesn't want another storage tier to manage.

metric_name: Likelihood to purchase low-cost disk in next 12 months (7-point scale)
notes: HP and EMC discussed; not purchased; not planning to. Management complexity is primary barrier.

metric_name: Primary barrier to low-cost disk adoption
metric_value: Administrative management overhead exceeds disk cost savings
notes: KEY FINDING: 'With tight budgets and decreasing headcount, he does not dare to introduce another level of disks to manage.' This is the central paradox of the early midline market.

metric_name: Relative cost: disk hardware vs. administration
metric_value: Disk costs are small compared to administrative costs
notes: Visionary insight for 2003; predicted that storage software/automation market would be larger than hardware savings. Drove ILM, HSM, and eventually cloud storage adoption.

metric_name: Required conditions to sell tiered storage to 'the Bobs'
notes: Kastner conclusion: 'Transparency and significant cost savings would be the requirement to sell the Bobs of the world.' Defines the automation + savings requirement for the segment.

metric_name: Storage tiering adoption by SMB/cost-constrained shops without automation tools
metric_value: Low without automation; management complexity will block adoption
notes: [DEFERRED actual outcome] Proved correct: tiering adoption in SMB was minimal until policy-based automation tools (StorageTek, Symantec/Veritas, NetApp ONTAP tiering) arrived ~2008-2012.

metric_name: Actual outcome: HCI/cloud as solution to tiering complexity
notes: [UNVERIFIED] Hyperconverged infrastructure (Nutanix 2011, VMware vSAN 2014) and cloud object storage (S3) solved the 'Bob problem' by eliminating tier management entirely.

metric_value: Headcount being reduced; must reduce complexity
notes: Direct driver of anti-tiering posture. Lean-IT pressure that would accelerate through 2008 financial crisis.

metric_value: Flat or declining; trying to reduce
notes: 3-year equipment lease cycle; operating budget; no TCO/ROI analysis performed.
On Unix; HP FC60 direct-attached; dedicated array; no performance bottleneck; no formal SLA.

metric_value: HP FC60 DAS; array dedicated to application; FC interface
notes: No shared SAN for primary OLTP; siloed architecture increases management burden.
Windows on SAN; Compaq SAN unit; email retained indefinitely — 'problems of cleaning it up are more costly than new disk.'

metric_value: Cannot afford staff time to clean up; new disk cheaper than cleanup labor
notes: Directly corroborates disk-cost < admin-cost insight. Also foreshadows compliance-driven email archiving boom (2004-2010).
CAD/CAM images of watches; shared with vendors; backup problems explicitly noted — potential low-cost disk candidate.

metric_value: HP robotic tapes; daily incremental + weekly full; 4 generations (1 offsite)
notes: Standard tiered tape backup.

metric_value: Cannot guarantee all critical data can be restored; backup is operational burden; restore time too long
notes: Contrast with State Street: Timex has more severe backup problems yet still rejects tiered disk due to management overhead.

metric_value: Zero dedicated storage staff; responsibility spread across system admins
notes: No full-time storage people. Explains why tier management is particularly costly for Timex.
Absence of SLAs removes accountability driver for performance investment — favors cost-reduction but also reduces urgency for any storage change.

metric_name: Manageability as adoption barrier for Oracle Financials tier
metric_value: Issue of manageability would prevent movement to less expensive disk media
notes: Even for the most likely candidate application (inactive OLTP data), management complexity blocks migration.

metric_value: Active archiving = 'planned storage hierarching'; online archiving = historical archive; nearline = CD archiving; low-cost = CD/floppy
notes: Nearline/low-cost conflated with optical/floppy media; demonstrates deep education gap in mid-market.

metric_value: Compaq FC SAN for Windows workloads; DAS for Unix/Oracle
notes: Hybrid SAN+DAS architecture typical of mid-market 2003. SAN used for file/email, DAS for primary database.

metric_value: 3-year operating lease cycle; no capital purchase
notes: Lease cycle constraint means asset refresh is fixed; limits opportunistic low-cost disk adoption.

metric_value: 'The Bobs' — budget-constrained ops managers; complexity-averse; potential but unreachable without automation
notes: Kastner coins 'the Bobs' as shorthand for this large market segment. Requires transparent automated tiering to convert. Represents the majority of mid-market IT shops.
